coredata.dc:title: Efficient solid-state and dual-mode photoluminescence of carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals for multifunctional applications
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Aihua
Song, Feng
Yao, Weijing
Han, Yingdong
Song, Feifei
Wu, Wei
Ming, Chengguo
Ju, Dandan
Khan, Adnan
coredata.dc:description: The carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals have been synthesized by the hydrothermal method and bright blue emission from carbon dots is observed in aqueous and solid state under 365 nm excitation. Self-quenching property of the carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals under solid state was deeply investigated, by changing the ratio of citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (DHE). The results indicate that the carbon dots can be effectively adsorbed and dispersed on the surface of NaLuF4 microcrystals, suppressing the aggregation of carbon dots. Furthermore, the upconversion luminescence can also be realized under 980 nm laser excitation. From the above observations, the carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals not only reveal emission property of carbon dots and NaLuF4, respectively, but also optimize the property of carbon dots. It provides a promising new strategy to prevent the carbon dots quenching in solid state and the carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals may have potential multi-applications in different fields including temperature sensing, solid-state display lighting as well as security inks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state luminescence
Carbon-dots
Upconversion luminescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 11674183 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin , key project (No. 17JCZDJC37800 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation mechanism of carbon dots/NaLuF4.

The SEM images of (a) sample 1, (b) sample 2, (c) sample 3 and (d) sample 4. (e1)-(e6) EDX elemental mappings of sample 1. (f) TEM, (g) High-resolution TEM and (h) EDX images of sample 1. (i1)-(i4) XRD patterns of sample1, sample 2, sample 3 and sample 4. The standard data of β-NaLuF4 (JCPDS No:27-0726) is at the bottom.

(a) High-resolution XPS full spectra; (b) the corresponding XPS C1s spectrum; (c) the corresponding XPS N1s spectrum; (d) the corresponding XPS O1s spectrum of the NaLuF4, sample 1 and sample 4, respectively.

(a) The upconversion emission spectra of sample 1, sample 2, sample 3 and sample 4; (b) comparisons of 655 nm, 550 nm, 522 nm and 409 nm emissions integrated emission intensities in NaLuF4 and sample 4; (c)–(d) pump power dependence of the upconversion luminescences in NaLuF4 and sample 4, respectively; (e) proposed energy transfer mechanism.

(a1)-(d2) Photographs of the sample 1, sample 2, sample 3 and sample 4 under daylight and UV light (365 nm). (e) Emission spectra of the sample 1, sample 2, sample 3 and sample 4 under 365 nm excitation. (f) Emission spectra of sample 4 upon excitation at different wavelengths.

(a) Luminescence spectra of sample1 and sample 4 under solid-state and liquid. (b) Photographs of sample 1 and sample 4 (0.0035 g/1 mL) in water solution under visible and UV light. (c) and (d) Dependence of luminescence intensity of sample 1 and sample 4 under 365 nm excitation in different volume water and ultrasonic time.

(a)–(c) Temperature-dependent upconversion luminescence spectra of β-NaLuF4:20 Yb3+/2Er3+ under 980 nm excitation (laser power = 1W (a), 1.5W (b), 2W (c)). (d) Monolog plots of Ln(R) as a function of inverse absolute temperature. (e) Relationship between R and the absolute temperature. (f) The sensing sensitivity as a function of absolute temperature.
(a) Temperature-dependent luminescence spectra and (b) the intensity under 365 nm excitation at the temperature ranging from 120 K to 300 K.

(a1), (a2) and (a3) The “NK” loaded on the PMMA using solid state carbon dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals under natural light, a 980 nm laser and 365 nm UV light; (b1), (b2) and (b3) The logo of Nankai loaded on a fluorescer-free paper using carbon dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals under natural light, a 980 nm laser and 365 nm UV light, respectively.
Atomic ratios of C, N, O, F, Na in XPS results.

head.ce:title: Efficient solid-state and dual-mode photoluminescence of carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals for multifunctional applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Printable Functional Nanomaterials and Printed Electronics, School of Printing and Packaging, Wuhan University, 430072, Wuhan, Hubei Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Printable Functional Nanomaterials and Printed Electronics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, School of Sciences, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, Weijin Road 94, Tianjin, 300222, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals have been synthesized by the hydrothermal method and bright blue emission from carbon dots is observed in aqueous and solid state under 365 nm excitation. Self-quenching property of the carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals under solid state was deeply investigated, by changing the ratio of citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (DHE). The results indicate that the carbon dots can be effectively adsorbed and dispersed on the surface of NaLuF4 microcrystals, suppressing the aggregation of carbon dots. Furthermore, the upconversion luminescence can also be realized under 980 nm laser excitation. From the above observations, the carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals not only reveal emission property of carbon dots and NaLuF4, respectively, but also optimize the property of carbon dots. It provides a promising new strategy to prevent the carbon dots quenching in solid state and the carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals may have potential multi-applications in different fields including temperature sensing, solid-state display lighting as well as security inks.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, carbon dots have attracted wide attention due to huge prospects in various fields such as bioimaging [1–3], display lighting devices [4,5] and sensors [6–8]. Compared with rare-earth ion-based phosphors and colloidal semiconductor quantum dots, the carbon dots show excellent characters attributed to good solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity and cost-effectiveness [9,10]. Ever since it was reported in 2004 [11], there have been a lot of methods to prepare the carbon dots, including hydrothermal carbonization, electrochemical synthesis, microwave pyrolysis and so on [12–17]. The most popular method is hydrothermal carbonization because of the eco-friendly reaction, high quantum yield and rich starting materials. Zhu et al. [18] have reported a facile and high quantum (yield is ca.58%) to obtain carbon dots with CA and DHE, which was suitable for industrial-scale production. However, the luminescence quenching is so easy to occur in solid states carbon dots owing to the aggregation [19–22], which limits the applications in solid-state displays. To prevent luminescence quenching of carbon dots in solid states, the carbon dots are generally fixed in matrices such as PVA, PMMA or PVP [23,24]. Recently, Shen et al. [25] reported that the long alkyl chains on the surface of carbon dots in the solid-state could suppress the aggregation-introduced luminescence quenching. Besides another report [21] also claimed that the hydroxyl groups of starch particles could adsorb carboxyl and amide groups of carbon dots to prevent the non-radiative decay and quenching introduced by aggregation. However, the carbon-based solid-state materials are still limited.

In addition, NaLnF4 (Ln = Lu, Y, Gd) material is an excellent upconversion luminescence material and has been widely investigated in recently years. In our previous paper [26], we have prepared NaLuF4 through a facile hydrothermal method with strong luminescence. The strategy of upconversion luminescence in NaLuF4 has been widely reported and achieved downconversion luminescence through using special rare earth elements, including Eu3+ or Tb3+ ions [27,28]. A new paper reported [29] that coating carbon dots on the surface of RE3+-doped materials displayed blue color fluorescence and green color under 365 nm as well as 980 nm excitation. However, this paper just concentrates on dual-mode luminescence in different rare earth pairs such as Er3+-Yb3+, Tm3+-Yb3+ and Er3+-Tm3+ but doesn't detect the properties of carbon dots. Moreover, the carbon dots in solid states usually can't emit under 365 nm excitation. Solid-state luminescence of the materials is crucial for future applications. Thus, it is attracting immense interest to develop a new and environmentally friendly material which can effectively emit both in solid states and aqueous solutions.

Herein, a series of carbon-dots/NaLuF4 phosphors have been effectively synthesized by the hydrothermal method by changing the ratios of CA and DHE. Intriguingly, the samples with different ratios of CA and DHE exhibit different properties, especially self-quenching-resistant property in solid state. The corresponding results were investigated in depth. What's more, the carbon-dots/NaLuF4 sample can display blue color luminescence both in aqueous solution and solid state under 365 nm UV lamp excitation as well as green color luminescence under 980 nm excitation. Obtaining such materials was considered to be important for understanding the properties of carbon dots, and would also find a lot of practical applications such as temperature sensing, solid-state display lighting and security inks.

The used Yb(NO3)3·6H2O (99.99%), Lu(NO3)3·6H2O (99.99%) and Er(NO3)3·6H2O (99.99%) were supplied by HWRK Chemical Company. NaNO3 and NH4F were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chem. and Tianjin Chem., respectively. Citric acid (CA) (98%) and ethylenediamine (DHE) (99%) were purchased from Tci (shanghai) Development Company and Aladdin, respectively. All the chemicals were of analytic grade and used as received without further purification.

First, theβ-NaLuF4:20 Yb3+/2Er3+ microcrystals (mol %) were prepared by a hydrothermal method [26]. The rare-earth aqueous solution were added to the sodium citrate tribasic dehydrate (2.941 g) with 10 mL water and stirred for about 30min. And then NaNO3 (0.085 g) and NHF4 (1M) were successively added to the above solution. The resulting mixture was transferred into a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 2 h. The resulting products were isolated by centrifugation and washed with deionized water as well as ethanol three times. The carbon-dots/NaLuF4 phosphors were synthesized by a previously reported process with a modification [29]. Sample 1: 1.051 g of CA was stirred in water (10 mL) for about 10min and 335 μL of DHE is added into the above solution. Subsequently, 0.2 g of NaLuF4 was added for 20 min under vigorous stirring, then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C, thereafter kept 200 °Cfor 5 h. The resulting products were collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol for several times, and dried at 80 °C for 12 h. Four species of carbon-dots/NaLuF4 were synthesized by tuning the ratios of CA and DHE [10]. NaLuF4: without adding any CA and DHE. Sample 1: 1.051 g of CA were reacted with 335 μL of DHE. Sample 2: 0.42 g of CA were reacted with 268 μL of DHE. Sample 3: 0.42 g of CA were reacted with 536 μL of DHE. Sample 4: 0.42 g of CA were reacted with 1275 μL of DHE.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by the D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Riagaku Company). The morphology of the products and the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) elemental mappings were observed with a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JEOL JSM-7500F). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were performed using a Tecnai G2 F20. The upconversion, downconversion emission spectra and decay curves of the samples were carried on a Horiba Fluorolog-3 luminescence spectrometer using a continuous 980 nm laser and a Xenon lamp as the excitation sources, respectively. Besides, the sample 4 at the range of 120 K–300 K and the quantum yield were recorded on a Nanolog luminescence spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra are performed on an Axis Ultra DLD and TENSOR II FTIR spectrometer, respectively. Raman spectra were recorded on a microscopic confocal Raman spectrometer (Renishaw inVia). The photographs under NIR and UV irradiation were read out using a Nikon D5200 camera. All the measurements were performed at room temperature.

Fig. 1 displays the synthesis and the solid state luminescence process of carbon dots/NaLuF4. Firstly, the NaLuF4 microcrystals are prepared by the hydrothermal method. Then, the as-prepared NaLuF4 microcrystals react with CA and DHE. During this reaction process, CA and DHE produce carbon dots and then cover the surface of NaLuF4. The carbon dots/NaLuF4 under solid state can be excited by 980 nm and 365 nm, emitting green and blue light, respectively.

After the synthesis, the SEM images of sample 1, sample 2, sample 3 and sample 4 are observed in Fig. 2 (a)-(d) to characterize the morphology. From sample 1 to sample 4, we can see that the samples almost have the same size distribution but the edges of NaLuF4 microcrystals are gradually corroded, which are ascribed to the corrosion from CA and DHE. Fig. 2(e1)-(e6) present the EDX elemental mappings of sample 1. Because the conductive adhesive composition has some C element, the elemental mapping of C covers the whole image and the Na, Lu, F, Er as well as Yb also distribute homogeneously in the whole sample, confirming the successful doping of C element. The TEM and HRTEM images of sample 1 are shown in Fig. 2(f) and (g). Obviously, we can see some dark dots with size about 5 nm on the surface of NaLuF4, implying that the carbon dots can disperse on the surface of NaLuF4. The EDX pattern of sample 1 from the same area is displayed in Fig. 2(h), further verifying the successful doping of C element in the NaLuF4. The concentration of C element in the samples will be discussed later in detail in the following parts of XPS spectra. In addition, Fig. 2 (d) exhibits the XRD patterns of the samples as well as standard data of pure β-NaLuF4 (JCPDS No: 27-0726). As shown, the diffraction patterns can be well indexed to β-NaLuF4. However, the diffraction patterns of the carbon dots aren't determined, maybe due to low reaction temperature or low concentration.

Moreover, the surface groups and chemical compositions were investigated by FTIR and XPS analysis. When adding the CA and DHE, some new IR peaks appear and the relative intensities of the different peaks change, as shown in Fig. S1. The FT-IR absorption peak especially at 1651 cm−1(CO/CN) decreases from sample 1 to sample 4, similar to the previous report [29]. The full XPS spectrums of NaLuF4, sample 1 and sample 4 are used to further detect the property, as displayed in Fig. 3 (a). Compared with NaLuF4, the intensity of element peaks of samples 1 and 4 at 1071.5eV (Na1s) and 684.5eV (F1s) are decreased as well as a new element peak appears at about 400.0 eV (N1s). In the high resolution spectrum of C1s peak (Fig. 3(b)), it can be de-convoluted into three peaks of sample 1 and 4, representing CO/CN, CN and CC/CC, respectively. The N1s fitting peak (Fig. 3(c)) only occurs in samples 1 and 4, containing one peak due to amino N. As can be seen from Fig. 3(d), the O1s fitting peak of NaLuF4 is different from samples 1 and 4. For samples 1 and 4, the fitting peaks correspond to CO and CO, respectively [30]. Those results are consistent with the carbon dots reported previously [18,29], which indicates that the presence of the carbon dots on the surface of the NaLuF4. Notably, the XPS intensity of element peaks are various for samples [Fig. S2], implying that the concentrations of related elements are different. The related results of all samples are shown in Table 1 . The concentration of C and N gradually decrease while O, F and Na increase from sample 1 to sample 4, which are in good agreement with the FTIR spectroscopy. As demonstrated in Table 1, the atomic ratio of carbon decrease from 73.09% to 54.01% while the oxygen increases from 11.50% to 22.71% from sample 1 to sample 4, exhibiting that the degree of oxidation increases. It is worth noting that the concentration of N is zero for sample 4 in Table 1, however, as shown in Fig. 3(c), a weak N1s is observed. This may be because the concentration of N is so low that its influence and content can be ignored while calculating the results. As a supplement, Raman spectrum of sample 4 is shown in Fig. S3. The intensity of the D band is stronger than G band which verifies that there are a lot of surface defect states in the carbon dots [31,32].

Fig. 4 (a) presents the upconversion spectra of solid-state sample 1, sample 2, sample 3 and sample 4 under 980 nm excitation. All samples reveal that there are characteristic emission peak positions at 522 nm, 541 nm and 655 nm of Er3+ ions, which can be assigned to the 2H11/2 → 4I15/2, 4S3/2 → 4I15/2 and 4F9/2 → 4I15/2 transitions [33], respectively, yielding a green color output. Additionally, a very week blue emission centered at 408 nm can be found (Fig. S4), originating from the 2H9/2 → 4I15/2 transition. The luminescence intensity significantly increases, reaching maximum in sample 3, and then slightly decreases. In order to investigate the influence of carbon dots, the integral intensity in sample 4 and NaLuF4 sample are shown in Fig. 4(b). All the emission bands slightly decrease in sample 4 with respect to the NaLuF4 sample (Fig. S4). And the log-log plots of them are measured as illustrated in Fig. 4(c) and (d). The transitions of 2H11/2 → 4I15/2 and 4F9/2 → 4I15/2 are changed from two-photon upconversion processes to two/three-photon while the 4S3/2 → 4I15/2 is the reverse process. Fig. S5 also shows the decay curves of the 4S3/2 → 4I15/2 (541 nm) and 4F9/2 → 4I15/2 (655 nm) transitions of Er3+ under 980 nm excitation in NaLuF4 and sample 4. Compared to the NaLuF4, the life time of sample 4 decreases for 4S3/2 → 4I15/2 (541 nm) and 4F9/2 → 4I15/2 (655 nm) transitions. According to the above-mentioned log-log plots results and the lifetime results, a possible reason is that more non-radiative transitions occur due to introducing surface defect when doping carbon dots. Therefore, more Er3+ ions relax non-radiatively to the long-lived 4I13/2 state, which facilitates the population of 4I13/2 and 4F9/2 levels of Er3+ ions [34–36]. However, the 2H11/2 and 4S3/2 energy levels may compete with each other, leading to the opposite trend. Also it makes a comparison between sample 2 and sample 4 to explore the influence of DHE, as shown in Figs. S6 and S7. The log-log slope values are similar (Fig. 4(d) and Fig. S6(b)), indicating that the upconversion mechanism isn't changed with various DHC. So, it seems that the enhancement of upconversion luminescence is more likely related to the amounts of DHE. Hence, the upconversion process mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 4(e).

The emission spectra of solid-state carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals are revealed in Fig. 5 (e) under 365 nm excitation. To our delight, the sample 3 and sample 4 exhibit self-quenching-resistant blue color fluorescence in solid state. From Fig. 5(a1) to Fig. 5(d1), it is noteworthy that the color of samples are brown, hazel, creamy white and white under daylight, respectively, indicating the carbonization degree of the original materials, agrees well with the results of Table 1. Meanwhile, Fig. 5(f) exhibits the emission spectra of sample 4 where the luminescence peak at 450 nm has no significant shift by changing the excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 380 nm, which confirmed that solid-state carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals remain the excitation-independent [24]. The quantum yield of sample 4 for the blue emission at 365 nm excitation is 7.45% in solid state.

To explore the solid-state resistance against quenching property of carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals, we consider the influence of surface states and aggregation of the carbon dots. Some previous papers [37–39] reported that a higher degree of oxidation on the carbon dots implies more surface defects, eventually changing the surface states. In addition, it is generally agreed that the surface states effectively control the luminescence of carbon dots, namely, the carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals. According to the FTIR and XPS spectrum, the surface states have some differences, which means that the influence of the surface states should not be neglected. Another important influence factor is aggregation of carbon dots. Usually, the carbon dots under solid state show strong luminescence quenching, due to the non-radiative decay processes and photoluminescence quenching induced by aggregation. Hence, we propose a strategy that the carbon dots disperse on the surface of NaLuF4 to prevent the aggregation. However, not all the samples in our experiment can luminesce in solid state. To acquire a deeper understanding of the solid-state luminescence property, the sample 1 and sample 4 are chosen as the model for investigation. The emission spectra of the sample 1 and sample 4 in aqueous solution (0.0036 g/1 mL) as well as solid state are displayed in Fig. 6 (a). The emission spectra of sample 4 in aqueous solution is almost same as that in solid state form. However, it can be seen that a broad emission band appears in sample 1 in aqueous solution with a strong cyan color (Fig. 6 (b2)), which is ascribed to that the carbon dots on the surface of NaLuF4 can effectively disperse in aqueous solution to resist the aggregation. The character of carbon-dots/NaLuF4 samples are further investigated in different volume water and ultrasonic time under 365 nm excitation as shown in Fig. 6 (c) and (d). For sample 1, with increasing the ultrasonic time and keeping the water volume constant, the intensity decreases, that is to say more carbon dots dropped from the NaLuF4 and thus cause lower emission intensity. While with further adding the water and maintaining the ultrasonic time, the intensity increases, indicating that at the 240s ultrasonic time in 1 mL water, the concentration of carbon dots in water has already reached the quenching concentration, and that with adding more water, the carbon dots dispersed in the water decrease, leading to the emission of carbon dots enhanced. These results manifest that a lot of carbon dots absorbed on the surface of NaLuF4 in sample 1. However, the photoluminescence intensity exhibits an obvious opposite trend in sample 4 (Fig. 6(d)). It is confirmed that the carbon dots on the surface of sample 4 are less than sample 1 in solid state, namely, the carbon dots dispersed on the surface don't aggregate for sample 4, thus leading to the self-quenching-resistant character under solid state. These observations are consistent with the results of XPS. Therefore, our strategy can effectively disperse the carbon dots on the surface of NaLuF4 to overcome self-quenching of carbon dots in solid state.

The temperature-dependent property is very important to the materials. Moreover, a lot of previous reports have shown that temperature sensing is realized via the thermally coupled levels (2H11/2 and 4S3/2) of Er3+ ions [40–42], which is regarded as a good candidate in promising applications such as temperature sensor. Although the luminescence of sample 4 is weaker than that NaLuF4 microcrystals, luminescent intensity can still meet requirements of temperature sensing. Therefore, the temperature-dependent luminescence of sample 4 under 980 nm excitation (laser power = 1W, 1.5W and 2W) was measured at temperatures ranging from 120 K to 300 K, as shown in Fig. 7 (a), (b) and (c). With the increase of temperature, the emission band intensity of 520 nm (512 nm-535 nm) increases, while the emission band of 540 nm (535 nm-570 nm) decreases, due to Boltzmann-type distribution. According to Boltzmann's equation, the fluorescence intensity ratio (R) can be written as follows [26]:
(1)
R
(I
520 nm
/I
540 nm
) =
I
520 nm
I
540 nm
= Cexp( -
ΔE
k
B
T
)
Where I520nm and I540nm are respectively the relative integrated intensities; C means the proportional constant; ΔE is the energy gap between the 520 nm and 540 nm; kB and T are the Boltzmann constant and the absolute temperature, respectively. It can be seen that the intensity ratio R(I520nm/R540nm) and LnR(I520nm/I540nm) are fitted well with relevant equations in Fig. 7(d) and (e) with high fitting degree values (r2). Sensitivity is also a worth investigating parameter, which can be defined as [40–42]:
(2)
S =
dR
dT
= R
ΔE
k
B
T
2

Elevated temperature leads to the increase of sensitivity as displayed in Fig. 7(f). The maximum sensitivity is about 0.0027 K−1 at 300 K under excitation pump power 1.5W. Compared with other materials in the previous literature, sample 4 has a relatively high sensitivity at 300 K, such as Yb3+/Er3+/Cr3+ doped transparent glass ceramics (∼0.0012 K−1) [40], ɑ-NaLuF4:Yb3+/Er3+ (∼0.0022 K−1) [41] and CaWO4:Yb3+/Er3+ (∼0.0008 K−1) [42]. Moreover, it is noted that the results obtained in three different pump powers could be fitted with almost the same curve in Fig. 7(d), (e) and (f), respectively. Hence, if using this material to detect the temperature, it has independency on the different powers, realizing of application easy for sensing temperature. To explain the above phenomenon, the detailed reason analysis will be discussed in future study. What's more, the temperature-dependent spectra of carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals under 365 nm excitation have also been investigated. With increasing the temperature, the luminescence intensity decreases under 365 nm excitation in Fig. 8 (a), attributed to the non-radiative recombination [21]. At the same time, due to electron-photon interaction, the luminescence peak displays a slight red shift as the temperature elevates. Obviously, the intensity decreases linearly at temperatures ranging from 120 K to 200 K, as shown in Fig. 8(b). This property can opportunely ensure the precision and guarantee self-calibrated temperature determination to make up the disadvantages of low sensitivity at 120 K–200 K when using intensity ratio R(I520nm/I540nm).

By taking advantage of the excellent optical performances about carbon-dots/NaLuF4, solid-state display lighting are shown in Fig. 9 (a1), (a2) and (a3). The solid-state carbon-dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals were embedded into a PMMA plate through laser ablation [43]. The labelled patterns (“NK”) present green emission under excitation of 980 nm while blue emission under the excitation of 365 nm. Thus, it is likely to enable application as light-emitting diodes. Currently, most anti-counterfeit technology are printed only use UV-to-visible inks such as currency, luxury items, tickets the important documents. Yet, it is becoming much easier to duplicate for counterfeiters. In response, combining UV-to-visible with NIR-to VIS of anti-counterfeit technology are proposed in our paper, which can provide a much higher security level against counterfeiting. To demonstrate our sample can be used as a security solution ink, a designable pattern, the logo of Nankai, is shown in Fig. 9(b1). In ambient lighting condition, the logo are almost invisible. As we expected, under the excitation of 980 nm and 365 nm, it can be obviously observed green and blue color by naked eyes in Fig. 9(b2) and (b3), respectively. So, this technique can quickly and massively print all kinds of patterns, paving the way for practical industrial production.

In conclusion, the carbon dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals were successfully synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method. By dispersing carbon dots on the surface of NaLuF4, dual-mode luminescence can be simultaneously achieved under 980 nm and 365 nm excitation, respectively. Moreover, the carbon dots/NaLuF4 microcrystals suppress the luminescence quenching of carbon dots, due to that the carbon dots can be effectively adsorbed and dispersed on the surface of NaLuF4 microcrystals, suppressing the aggregation. It is found that under various excitation powers of 980 nm (1W, 1.5W, 2W), the fitting results obtained could be fitted with almost the same curve, indicating that the sensitivity keeps unchanged in the different powers, which makes it easy to realize application for sensing temperature. The temperature-dependent property of carbon dots/NaLuF4 phosphors under 365 nm excitation reveals a linear relation at 120 K–200 K, which guarantees self-calibrated temperature determination to make up the disadvantages of low sensitivity when using intensity ratio R(I520nm/I540nm). We also propose that the carbon dots/NaLuF4 can be utilized as anti-fake printing in solid state and aqueous solution with dual-mode fluorescence, which provides a much higher level of security against counterfeiting. Due to excellent optical performances of carbon-dots/NaLuF4, it may provide new possibility for multi-applications in different fields including temperature sensing, solid-state display lighting as well as security inks.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 11674183) and Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin, key project (No. 17JCZDJC37800).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.10.125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots: a facile and general preparation method, photoluminescence investigation, and imaging applications
Truly fluorescent excitation-dependent carbon dots and their applications in multicolor cellular imaging and multidimensional sensing
One-step preparation of nitrogen-doped grapheme quantum dots from oxidized debris of graphene oxide
Intercrossed carbon nanorings with pure surface states as low-cost and environment-friendly phosphors for white-light-emitting diodes

Photoluminescent organosilane-functionalized carbon dots as temperature probes
One-step synthesis of multi-emission carbon nanodots for ratiometric temperature sensing
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots

Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Investigation into the fluorescence quenching behaviors and applications of carbon dots
